Patch Set 5:

Uh, whoops - Didn't expect that.  I pushed lock ahead (http://review.whamcloud.com/#/c/13564/) on a branch with this commit on it, and it created a new patch set here as well.  Makes some sense, I guess.  I double checked and there are no changes from the previous patch set.

Patch Set 5:

Code-wise looks good to me.

Patch Set 5: Code-Review+2

That happens if you have rebased your branch on master.  If the second patch has conflicts then this first patch also needs to be updated, otherwise the second patch won't be able to access the newer changes.

Oleg - (Replying here since comments are scattered across patch sets right now)  I guess I think it's worthwhile trying to conserve ioctl numbers.   Both because we don't have an infinite number and because the upstream kernel sometimes takes them from us.

Patch Set 8:

I think there's a misunderstanding on how those IOCTL numbers are created.
It's an automatic macro dependent on size of the structure, and other such stuff.

So if you change the ioctl structure argument, it WILL change the macro already for you.
So if your plan is to look at the magic and then determine what size of the struct was used, it's already a fail.
See the definition of _IOR used to define it:

#define LL_IOC_LADVISE                  _IOR('f', 250, struct lu_ladvise)

Patch Set 8:

so I guess just to restate, I think it makes 100% sense to just copy the whole struct in and then check the magic and other field and then pull in whatever else might be there.
the struct size is guaranteed to be what you think it is.

In the future when you would change the structure - that would also change your IOCTL number so you would need to have a copy of the old structure around to handle old callers and have two ioctl handlers.

(1 comment)

If you just want the structures to be the same - it's ok to leae tehm in the old place AND ad them to the wirecheck to ensure nothing changes.
then leave a comment about ioctl at the top of the structure definition so that whoever changes it keeps in ind the implications.

If you want to futureproof it a bit then you want to add code in llite/file.c to get the llapi version of the struct and then copy the data (After verifying) into the wire struct before sending, that way they are totally decoupled even if they are the same at first.

Patch Set 18:

Hm... What happened to the plan of redefining LL_IOC_LADVISE
from _IOR('f', 250, struct lu_ladvise) to the new llapi version?
I know hthey are teh same now so it's not a big deal, but we probably need to do it before it's forgotten?
Is there a separate patch for that?

Patch Set 18:

Since this API has never been in any release, there is no reason to change the IOC or MAGIC numbers for this change. It is also compatible with the old API on little-endian machines, so I don't think there is a need to change it.

Patch Set 18: -Code-Review

I think Oleg is referring to the structure definition used in the ioctl.  It won't actually change the effective number since they're the same, but it *is* the wrong structure name in there.

Seems wrong to me.  I'll rebase unless you still think not, Andreas.

Patch Set 18:

I think refreshing the patch is the most correct thing to do here.
If you want to just do a separate patch to fix the definition - that's fine by me as well, though a bit less preferred.

Patch Set 7:

Every time when I find a patch like this which waits months before
finally being merged, I can't help thinking how much time the
developer has spent on checking test results, rebasing the codes,
pinging reviewers, and then waiting or giving up. And if the patch
has been merged a lot ealier, using that much time, the developer
could work on imporving the existing codes, as well as a lot of
other stuff. I understand that if the patch change something that
is very cirtical, it is really necessary to keep cautious beforing
merging it. However, if the patch is just adding a new feature used
by nobody yet, and doesn't change anything in the normal I/O path,
I think it is reasonably safe to merge the patch after a acceptable
test and review. Given the fact that test and review is not so fast,
it might be necessary to apply a relatively less strict merging
policy for patches of new features. After all, it is just impossible
to prevent from merging patches with new defects, and it is hard to
predict whehter a new feature will be widely (or ever) used or not
before it is already there.

parents.subject: LU-7800 llog: check if next llog exists

commit.subject: LU-7225 llite: ladvise protocol changes
commit.message:
LU-7225 llite: ladvise protocol changes

This patch makes some changes to the ladvise API and
protocol to support lock ahead and possible future users.

Primarily, it separates the userspace API arguments from
the structures which go out on the network, and adds a
number of 'value' fields without a predefined use.

The meaning of each value field can be different for
different advice types, allowing some extensibility.

Signed-off-by: Patrick Farrell <paf@cray.com>
